coredata.dc:title: Micro-nanobubble-assisted TiO2/ZnO/rGO nanocomposites for efficient pollutant degradation and antibacterial activity

coredata.dc:description: In this work, TiO2/ZnO/rGO (TZR) nanocomposites (NCs) were prepared by hydrothermal methods and systematically evaluated for their photocatalytic and antibacterial activity, aiming at efficient environmental remediation. The physicochemical characteristics of the acquired samples were examined by the use of several methods, including UV–visible spectroscopy, X-ray Diffraction (XRD), X-ray Photo electron Spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Scanning Electron Microscopy with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (SEM- EDX), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Ultraviolet-Visible Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (UV-DRS), and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis, which confirmed the successful combination of TiO2, ZnO, and rGO within the composite structure. Photocatalytic performance exposed that the TZR NCs, particularly when combined with micro-nanobubbles (MNB), exhibited notable degradation efficiencies of 99.8 % for Indigo Carmine (IC), 98.73 % for Reactive Black 5 (RB5), and 99.60 % for carbaryl pesticide within 120 min under UVA light irradiation. The presence of micro-nanobubbles (MNB) significantly enhanced dissolved oxygen concentration and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, accelerating the pollutant degradation process. Furthermore, the TZR NCs exhibited notable antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus (S.aureus) at 12 mm for 75 μgL−1 and Escherichia coli (E.coli) at 10 mm for 75 μgL−1. This thorough study emphasizes the practical application of TZR NCs photocatalytic devices for effective wastewater treatment, providing a potential dual-action method for reducing both chemical pollutants and microbiological contaminants in aquatic environments.

head.ce:title: Micro-nanobubble-assisted TiO2/ZnO/rGO nanocomposites for efficient pollutant degradation and antibacterial activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, TiO2/ZnO/rGO (TZR) nanocomposites (NCs) were prepared by hydrothermal methods and systematically evaluated for their photocatalytic and antibacterial activity, aiming at efficient environmental remediation. The physicochemical characteristics of the acquired samples were examined by the use of several methods, including UV–visible spectroscopy, X-ray Diffraction (XRD), X-ray Photo electron Spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Scanning Electron Microscopy with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (SEM- EDX), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Ultraviolet-Visible Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (UV-DRS), and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis, which confirmed the successful combination of TiO2, ZnO, and rGO within the composite structure. Photocatalytic performance exposed that the TZR NCs, particularly when combined with micro-nanobubbles (MNB), exhibited notable degradation efficiencies of 99.8 % for Indigo Carmine (IC), 98.73 % for Reactive Black 5 (RB5), and 99.60 % for carbaryl pesticide within 120 min under UVA light irradiation. The presence of micro-nanobubbles (MNB) significantly enhanced dissolved oxygen concentration and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, accelerating the pollutant degradation process. Furthermore, the TZR NCs exhibited notable antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus (S.aureus) at 12 mm for 75 μgL−1 and Escherichia coli (E.coli) at 10 mm for 75 μgL−1. This thorough study emphasizes the practical application of TZR NCs photocatalytic devices for effective wastewater treatment, providing a potential dual-action method for reducing both chemical pollutants and microbiological contaminants in aquatic environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution has become a critical environmental issue in the 21st century, driven by industrial expansion, intensive agriculture, and insufficient wastewater management systems. These factors have degraded vital water resources, including rivers, lakes, and groundwater [1–3]. Industrial facilities often release untreated effluents containing hazardous chemicals and dyes, while agricultural practices add high pesticides and fertilizers to water bodies. Urbanization further burdens municipal wastewater systems, introducing new contaminants such as pharmaceutical residues and antibiotic-resistant bacteria that conventional treatment methods struggle to address [4–6]. Conventional treatment methods, such as adsorption, ultrafiltration, and biological processes, are frequently employed to remove contaminants from wastewater. However, these approaches necessitate further refinement to address the growing complexity of pollutants. Emerging contaminants, including synthetic dyes, pesticides, pharmaceuticals, and antibiotic-resistant bacteria, pose significant challenges due to their persistence and intricate molecular structures [7–9]. For example, while adsorption can capture these contaminants, it does not degrade them, and ultrafiltration only physically separates pollutants without breaking them down. Additionally, ultrafiltration's high energy requirements and the operational costs of biological treatments limit the overall effectiveness of traditional methods [10,11].

Photocatalysis emerges as a highly effective and sustainable approach for addressing environmental pollutants in wastewater. This method offers several benefits, including the use of sunlight as a renewable energy source, environmental friendliness, cost efficiency, strong capability to mineralize organic pollutants, and excellent long-term photostability, which enables repeated use without significant loss of activity [12,13]. Among various photocatalysts, metal oxides such as TiO2, ZnO, MnO2, Bi2O3, Ag2O and SnO2 have been widely investigated for their potential in wastewater treatment. Among them, TiO2 and ZnO stand out as particularly promising materials due to their suitable band gap energies, low toxicity, and high photosensitivity [14–18]. However, a major limitation of these metal oxides is the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole (e--h+) pairs, stemming from the short lifespan of these charge carriers, which significantly reduces their quantum efficiency. Additionally, both materials absorb only a narrow range of the light spectrum, further restricting their practical usage [19,20]. To overcome these limitations, coupling ZnO with TiO2 has been proposed as an effective strategy to enhance photocatalytic performance [21]. Previous studies have shown that ZnO-TiO2 nanocomposites exhibit significantly improved photocatalytic activity in dye degradation compared to the individual oxides.

Ternary nanocomposites have demonstrated remarkably enhanced catalytic performance compared to binary systems or individual photocatalysts. The synergistic interaction among the three distinct components can generate a greater number of active sites, thereby significantly boosting the efficiency of pollutant degradation. Moreover, ternary structures promote more effective charge separation and transfer, which are critical factors in improving photocatalytic performance. The combination of different materials provides efficient pathways for electron and hole migration, thereby minimizing charge carrier recombination and further enhancing the overall photocatalytic activity. Carbon-based materials such as graphene, graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and activated carbon have been widely utilized in wastewater treatment owing to their exceptional electrical conductivity and large surface area [22–25]. Particularly rGO exhibits unique optical, electrical, mechanical, and thermal properties that are highly beneficial for electron retention and transfer across its surface. As a result, rGO plays a vital role in promoting efficient electron-hole pair separation and significantly reducing their recombination rate, thereby enhancing photocatalytic performance [26]. The integration of rGO with TiO2 and ZnO presents a highly promising strategy, as it not only mitigates particle agglomeration and enhances the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers but also facilitates the efficient adsorption of organic pollutants and extends light absorption into the visible region. Consequently, the formation of TiO2/ZnO/rGO (TZR) heterojunctions significantly improves photocatalytic performance compared to individual materials. To the best of our knowledge, there is still a lack of comprehensive understanding regarding the synergistic effects arising from the combination of TiO2, ZnO, and rGO, particularly in terms of their collective role in enhancing photocatalytic activity and promoting pollutant degradation under longer wavelength visible light irradiation. Furthermore, the Micro-nano bubbles (MNB) greatly enhance photocatalytic performance through improved oxygen dissolution and increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation. MNB enhances dissolved oxygen levels compared to conventional systems, facilitating surface oxidation reactions on photocatalysts and speeding up degradation rates. The MNB is technology utilized in multiple applications, including the cleaning and descaling of solid surfaces, degradation of organic pollutants, removal of micropollutants, disinfection of water, and enhancement of photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants [25,27–29].

This study investigates the synthesis and application of advanced TZR nanocomposites (NCs) via hydrothermal methods for efficient photocatalytic degradation of wastewater pollutants. The NCs are tested on IC RB5 dyes, carbaryl pesticide, and antibacterial activity of Escherichia coli, and Streptococcus aureus. By combining MNB aeration with photocatalysis, the study enhances pollutant degradation, improves photoelectric interactions, stabilizes photocatalysts, and increases dissolved oxygen, promoting better water treatment. Additionally, kinetic modeling is used to explore the photocatalytic mechanisms, offering insights into optimizing and advancing water remediation technologies.

All chemicals used were of analytical grade (A.R.): TiO2 nano-powder (>95.0 %, SRL), ZnO (99.0 %, SRL), rGO (≥99.5 %, Nanografi), CH3OH (≥98.0 %, MACRON), IC dye, RB5 dye, and carbaryl pesticide, all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

Fig. S1 illustrates the preparation process for four types of photocatalysts: TiO2/ZnO (TZ), TiO2/rGO (TR), ZnO/rGO (ZR), and TiO2/ZnO/rGO (TZR). The initial mixtures were prepared as follows: the TZ photocatalyst was synthesized by combining 4 g of TiO2 and 4 g of ZnO, which were then stirred in 120 mL of deionized (DI) water for 30 min. The TR photocatalyst was prepared by mixing 4 g of TiO2 with 0.05 g of rGO, followed by vigorous stirring in 120 mL of DI water and ultrasonication for 30 min. The ZR photocatalyst was created using 4 g of ZnO and 0.05 g of rGO, stirred in 120 mL of DI water, and ultrasonicated for 30 min. The TZR photocatalyst was prepared with 4 g of TiO2, 4 g of ZnO, and 0.05 g of rGO, also stirred in 120 mL of DI water, and ultrasonicated for 30 min. To create a uniform suspension, all solutions were mixed and stirred for 60 min following the initial preparation. This suspension was then transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h, after which it was allowed to cool to room temperature. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 7 min to separate the solid phase, and the supernatant was discarded. The remaining powder was washed with DI water, followed by repeated stirring and centrifugation steps, and then dried at 70 °C for 12 h. Each prepared mixture underwent this single-step synthesis process to yield the final photocatalyst products. The final yield of the nanocomposite was 7.8 g, corresponding to a yield of approximately 96.25 %, based on the total initial precursor mass of 8.05 g.

This investigation meticulously delineates the synthesized materials utilizing sophisticated analytical methodologies, offering an in-depth comprehension of their structural, morphological, and electronic characteristics. X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku Smart Lab diffractometer) The catalyst crystal structure was determined using X with CuK radiation (40 kV, 40 mA, k = 1.5406 Å and 2θ range from 10 to 90°).in the Shimadzu XER-6100 model from Japan. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed utilizing a ULVAC-PHI instrument, specifically the PHI5000 model. The synthesized photocatalysts' Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were analyzed using a Brucker RFS27 spectrophotometer. The prepared photocatalysts' morphologies and micro/nanostructures were examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI Quanta FEG 200) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL JEM 2100). The photocatalyst's optical properties and band structure were analyzed using UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy with a Perkin Elmer Lambda 25 spectrometer and a Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) analyzer (BELSORP-max Bel Japan Inc.), Photoluminescence behavior was measured using photoluminescence spectrometer (HORIBA Scientific, model: FLUOROMAX PLUS), Intermediate metabolites was confirmed by Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC–MS) Model GC6890N-MS5973N, The photooxidation reaction in the degradation of dyes and carbaryl contaminants in synthetic wastewater was also confirmed by examining the residual total organic carbon (TOC) content. The synthetic wastewater samples were analyzed using a TOC analyzer, Analytikjena, model 3100, HT1300 solids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation performance of three different groups of contaminants
ce:section.ce:para: Three distinct experimental setups were used to evaluate the performance of dye and pesticide degradation in the photocatalytic process. The first setup (Experiment sets 1–4) utilized the RMUTT-MNB generator to introduce dissolved oxygen through micro-nano bubbles (MNB) into synthetic wastewater, achieving DO levels of 7.67–8.14 mgL−1 for 15 min before initiating the experiment. The Malvern model NS300 was utilized to analyze the size, size distribution, and concentration of MNB particles in synthetic aerated wastewater. The second setup (Experiment sets 5–8) investigated the photocatalytic process without MNB addition. Finally, a control experiment (Experiment sets 9–10) was conducted to provide a clear comparison of the degradation performance under varying conditions. The dyes used in this study were IC and RB5, each with an initial concentration of 10 mgL−1, while the pesticide used was carbaryl at an initial concentration of 100 mgL−1. Table S1 defines the detailed experimental conditions.

The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized photocatalysts was assessed by evaluating the photocatalytic degradation of IC and RB5 dye. Batch experiments were performed using a 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask, which attends as the photoreactor (Fig. S2). The photoreactor was filled with 100 mL of synthetic dye wastewater containing either IC or RB5 at an initial concentration of 100 mgL−1, along with 0.1 g of the photocatalyst. UVA light (300 W, Osram) with a consistent intensity of 11.50 Wm−2 was applied to initiate the photocatalytic process. The light intensity inside the photoreactor was measured using a UV light meter (UV-340 model).

In experiment sets 1–4, MNB were incorporated into the synthetic dye wastewater during a 15-min preparation step, as previously described. In contrast, experiment sets 5–8, were prepared without adding MNB. The dye degradation efficiency was evaluated by comparing the results of a controlled experiment (Experimental Sets 9–10). Variations in the dye concentration within the synthetic wastewater were tracked over a 120-min reaction period. The dye concentrations were investigated using a spectrophotometer (Libra S12, visible and UV). Furthermore, gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS; model GC 6890 N, Agilent) was used to monitor and identify the molecular structural changes of the dyes that occurred during the photooxidation in photocatalytic process and resulted in the creation of intermediate products. An Agilent HP-5 ms capillary column, an Agilent G1888 blank sampler, and an MSD 5973 N Ion Source EI Single Quint were all included in the GC–MS system.

A similar experimental setup as the dye degradation studies was used to further evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized photocatalysts for the degradation of carbaryl. A standard carbaryl solution was prepared by dissolving 98.9 % pure carbaryl (LGC Standards Dr. Ehrenstorfer, CAS No. 63–25-2) in methanol and DI water to achieve the desired concentration of 10 mgL−1. The photoreactor was then filled with 100 mL of this synthetic wastewater containing carbaryl at the specified initial concentration. As previously described, experiment sets 1–4 included the incorporation of MNB during a 15-min preparation step, while sets 5–8 were prepared without MNB. Experimental Sets 9–10 were used to compare the results with those of the previous two experiment sets to determine the carbaryl degradation efficiency. The carbaryl concentration was measured throughout the 120-min experiment. At specific time intervals, carbaryl samples were collected and subjected to spectrophotometric analysis using a Hitachi U-3900 UV–visible spectrophotometer. The compound exhibited a characteristic absorption peak at 220 nm, which was utilized to determine the concentration of carbaryl residues in the synthesized wastewater samples.

The antibacterial activity of TZR photocatalyst was evaluated against Escherichia coli (E. coli) (ATCC 25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) (ATCC 29737) under visible light conditions. The study employed the well diffusion (WD) method to assess their antibacterial potential. A nutrient agar medium was prepared by dissolving 28 g of nutrient powder in 1000 mL of distilled water to support bacterial growth. Different concentrations of TZR NCs (25, 50, and 75 μgL−1) were weighed and dispersed in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) using one-hour sonication. Microbial inoculate, adjusted to 5 ×
10 5
CFUmL−1, were uniformly spread over the surface of Mueller Hinton Agar (MHA) plates. Wells were carefully created using a sterile cork borer, and 1 mL of TZR NCs solutions at the desired concentrations was introduced into the wells. Each plate included a well-treated with amoxicillin as a positive control and another with DMSO as a negative control. The tests were conducted in triplicate for each bacterial strain. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 18 h, and the diameters of inhibition zones were recorded in mm.

The XRD patterns of the synthesized TZ, TR, ZR, and TZR photocatalysts are presented in Fig. 1a. The diffraction peaks observed at 2θ values of 25°, 38°, 47.96°, 55.4°, and 54.7° correspond to the (101), (004), (200), (105), and (204) planes of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21–1272). Additionally, the peaks at 2θ values of 31.82°, 34.5°, 36.29°, 47.5°, 56.64°, 62.5°, 66.05°, and 67.66° can be attributed to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), and (112) crystal planes of ZnO (JCPDS No. 36–1451). Moreover, the XRD spectra for TZ, TR, and ZR exhibit a combination of characteristic peaks from TiO2 and ZnO nanoparticles, consistent with previous studies [30]. Fig. 1a presents the XRD spectra of the synthesized TZR photocatalysts, confirming the presence of both TiO2 and ZnO. The absence of a diffraction peak for rGO suggests either (1) the low concentration of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) in the nanocomposite or (2) the complete exfoliation of graphite. In cases of thorough exfoliation, the carbon-related diffraction peaks become significantly diminished or vanish entirely [31]. The XRD analysis verifies the crystal structures and phase purity of the synthesized nanoparticles and nanocomposites, further supporting these findings by previous studies [32].

Fig. 1b displays the infrared spectra of the prepared pure oxides rGO, TZ, TR, ZR, and TZR photocatalysts. The spectrum of rGO, exhibits characteristic peaks around 1591 cm−1 (CC stretching from sp2 carbon domains) and 2322 cm−1. The TZ, TR, ZR, and TZR photocatalysts spectra exhibit a broad and intense band around 397.96 cm−1, corresponding to the TiO and ZnO bonds, respectively [33]. This band is observed in the spectrum of the TZR photocatalysts. Additionally, the band at 1591.0 cm−1 forms M-O-C bonds, indicating the linkage between the rGO sheets and the metal oxides [34]. Overall, characteristic bands corresponding to TZ, TR, ZR, and TZR in the synthesized TZR photocatalysts confirm its successful formation. Notably, the FTIR spectra of TZR samples exhibit a broad peak of around 3448 cm−1, which can be attributed to OH stretching vibrations [35]. The absence of additional peaks in the spectra confirms the successful formation of the pure TZR photocatalysts.

XPS spectroscopy was employed to analyze the quantitative surface chemical composition and oxidation states of the synthesized ZnO-TiO2/rGO photocatalysts, as illustrated in Fig. 2 (a-e). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2a) clearly revealed the presence of Zn 2p, Ti 2p, O 1 s, and C 1 s on the surface of the ZnO-TiO2/rGO photocatalysts. In Fig. 2b, the Zn 2p spectrum shows two prominent peaks at 1023 eV and 1044.22 eV, corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively [36]. Similarly, the Ti2p spectrum (Fig. 2c) exhibits a doublet with peaks at 457.66 eV and 463.90 eV, representing the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 orbital states [37]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2d) features three distinct peaks at 530.09 eV and 531.47 eV. The peak at 531.47 eV corresponds to lattice oxygen in TiO2, while the peak at 530.09 eV is attributed to surface defects, oxygen vacancies, and the OH group covalently bonded to Ti4+. Additionally, the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2e) shows three peaks at 284.19 eV, 285.05 eV, and 286.40 eV. These XPS findings are consistent with previous reports [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of surface morphology and elemental composition
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 (a, b) presents the SEM images of the TZR NCs. The micrographs provide a clear view of the overall surface morphology of the samples. TZR displays an irregular shape with agglomerated structures, while the rGO nanoparticles are uniformly distributed across the surface of the TZR nanoparticles [38]. The TZR photocatalyst was confirmed through energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS), with the corresponding weight percentage (wt%) results it can confirm the occurrence of carbon (C), oxide (O), titanium (Ti), and zinc (Zn) elements, which are the constituents of the NCs 16.65, 28.54, 36.84, and 17.97 %, and the measured atomic percentage of 32.89, 42.33, 18.25 and 6.52 % respectively [39].

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) can further assess the particle size, distribution, and morphology. Fig. 4 presents the HR-TEM images of TZR samples, revealing that both TZ and rGO nanoparticles are well incorporated within the nanocomposite. Additionally, the micrographs indicate noticeable agglomeration of TZ and rGO particles. The TEM images also confirm that the TZ nanoparticles are effectively distributed across the rGO nanosheets [40]. The HR-TEM images (Fig. 4c) revealed two distinct lattice fringes. One set of fringes had a spacing of 0.282 nm, which corresponds to the (101) plane of ZnO. The other set showed a fringe spacing of 0.352 nm, attributed to the interplanar distance of the (110) plane in anatase TiO2 [41]. These HR-TEM findings align well with the XRD results, confirming the formation of the TiO2/ZnO composite. The SAED pattern of TZR depicted in (Fig. 4 (d)) illustrates various bright spots from this ring formation reveals that the particles are in the polycrystalline [42].

Fig. S3(a-b) illustrates that the BET-specific surface area is vital for various applications, particularly in photocatalysis. The composite surface area, pore volume, and porosity were assessed through nitrogen adsorption/desorption tests using BET analysis. The TZR NCs demonstrated a specific surface area of 74.729 m2g−1, and a pore volume of 0.126 ccg−1 and a pore diameter of 7.274 nm indicates a mesoporous structure. The shape of the nitrogen adsorption isotherm suggests that the structural integrity is maintained and that microspores are present within the photocatalysts. A large surface area in photocatalysts enhances the interaction between the photocatalyst and the analyte, thus improving photocatalytic efficiency [43].

Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) was employed to analyze the light absorption characteristics of the synthesized photocatalyst samples. Fig. 5a displays the DRS spectra for TZ, TR, ZR, and TZR NCs. The results indicate that the incorporation of rGO significantly influences the optical properties of the TZR NCs. As the rGO content decreases, there is a notable increase in absorbance within the visible light region, spanning 400 to 700 nm. TZ, ZR, and TZR NCs exhibit similar absorption profiles, featuring a sharp absorption edge in the 450–477 nm wavelength range, which correlates with the band gap energy of the samples [44]. The Tauc plot was used to calculate the bandgap energy (Eg) of the TZ, TR, ZR, and TZR (NCs); the findings are shown in Fig. 5a.

Fig. 5b presents the bandgap values for the synthesized TZ, TR, ZR, and TZR samples. The values obtained were 3.07 eV, 3.10 eV, 3.03 eV, and 2.93 eV, respectively, all lower than those for TiO2 (3.20 eV) and ZnO (3.37 eV). The reduction in bandwidth enhances electron excitation when exposed to visible light irradiation, thereby improving photocatalytic activity [31].

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were utilized to analyze the behavior of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the TR, ZR, and TZR samples. These spectra offer crucial information on carrier dynamics, including trapping, migration, and interfacial charge transfer, which play a vital role in improving photocatalytic performance. Fig. S4 shows that the PL spectra of TR, ZR, and TZR NCs have an emission peak of about 625 nm, which corresponds to the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [45]. The PL intensity provides insight into the recombination rate, where a lower intensity indicates enhanced charge separation and improved photocatalytic activity. Among the samples, TZR shows the lowermost PL intensity, suggesting that the combination of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) effectively suppresses electron-hole recombination by facilitating charge transfer. In contrast, TR and ZR NCs show higher PL intensities, indicating a relatively higher recombination rate, which may lead to lower photocatalytic efficiency. The highest PL intensity observed in TR suggests that this composition has the least effective charge separation. TZR lower PL intensity emphasizes its higher potential as a photocatalyst due to the synergistic interaction of TiO2, ZnO, and rGO, which promotes charge carrier dynamics and contributes to better photocatalytic performance in dye and pesticide degradation [46].

The particle size distribution of MNB in the samples was evaluated using a Malvern Panalytical Nano-Sight, which utilizes Nano-Tracking Analysis (NTA). The particle size was determined using hydrodynamic principles, with the Stokes-Einstein equation employed to assess the MNB in the liquid phase. This study investigated the occurrence of MNB in aquatic settings produced by circulating water through an RMUTT-MNB generator. The average diameter of the MNB spheres was 100 nm, with dimensions varying from 53 to 731 nm. The physical study revealed that the mean bubble size of the MNB was 102.1 ± 1.0 nm. This apparatus produces MNB by aerating uniformly sized air droplets. The aeration of MNB resulted in a corresponding rise in DO levels. The dissolved oxygen levels varied between 7.67 and 8.14 mgL−1, reaching a stable equilibrium within approximately 15 min at a water temperature of 28 °C. Smaller MNB demonstrated excellent stability and sustained a longer length in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation efficiency of IC and RB5 dyes
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized TZ, TR, ZR, and TZR NCs catalysts were used to degrade in the commercial dyes IC and RB5 under UVA light. The prominent absorption peaks were determined to assess dye degradation through UV–visible spectroscopy. The absorption peaks of IC and RB5 at 610 nm and 600 nm, respectively, illustrated the dye intensity effectively. Table S2 presents the degradation efficiency of IC and RB5 dyes after 120 min of testing under conditions 1, 2, and 3, with an initial concentration of 100 mgL−1 of dye.

Fig. 6 (a-d) (I) presents the dye concentrations observed in photocatalysts in the presence and absence of MNB. The photocatalysts underwent evaluation for UVA-induced degradation of IC and RB5 dyes. Control studies indicated minimal degradation rates (<2 %) for IC and RB5, implying that direct photolysis alone is inadequate for dye degradation. The initial concentration of dye (C0) has a substantial impact on the efficiency of degradation. Elevated dye concentrations obstruct light from accessing the active sites of the photocatalyst, leading to a decrease in catalytic efficiency. Elevated dye concentrations can inhibit the formation of reactive oxygen species on the photocatalyst surface, including •OH and
O 2
∙ −
. The reduction in the synthesis of reactive species impedes degradation, as these species are responsible for breaking down color molecules. Without photocatalysts, the degradation of MNB or UVA is not substantial. Photocatalysts significantly enhanced degradation efficiency. The TZ, TR, and ZR photocatalysts exhibited degradation efficiencies of <92.28 % for IC dye and < 79.32 % for RB5 dye under identical conditions. When UVA light was irradiated on the TZR photocatalyst while MNB were present, both dyes were almost completely broken, with an efficiency of over 99 %. MNBs are not present in experiments assessing photodegradation efficiency. When UVA light was used, the IC dye with TZ, TR, and ZR photocatalysts had degradation efficiencies below 80.94 %, while the RB5 dye had degradation efficiencies below 79.32 %.

The photocatalytic performance of the TZR NCs with MNB was investigated for the degradation of IC and RB5 dyes under UVA irradiation. UV–visible spectroscopy was utilized to monitor the degradation process, with specific attention to the absorption peaks of IC and RB5 dyes at 610 nm and 600 nm, respectively. In experimental conditions 1 (TZR + UVA + MNB), IC and RB5 dye solutions were collected over 120-min to measure concentration changes. Fig. 6(a-d) (II) for the UV–vis spectrum of IC and RB5 dye solutions with TZR NCs and MNB added. As the irradiation time increased, the absorption peak intensity peaks of IC and RB5 decreased significantly. This shows that photocatalytic degradation was working well. The decrease in absorption intensity confirms the degradation of IC and RB5 dyes, highlighting the superior performance compared to all photocatalysts in this research.

The results show how essential MNB is for increasing the oxygen available, making mass transfer more straightforward, and creating ROS needed for dye degradation. This study indicates that MNB assisted photocatalysis is a practical and scalable way to clean up wastewater, especially industrial wastewater that contains long-lasting organic dyes. The results are detailed in Table S2.

MNB were produced steadily and significantly under experimental conditions 1 (TZR + UVA + MNB) in Table S2, enhancing photocatalytic degradation. The persistence of smaller MNB in water raised (DO) levels. This increased ROS such as H2O2, •OH, and •O2 −. As the principal oxidative agents, ROS degraded dyes. Photocatalysts accumulate dissolved organic matter (DOM), delaying deterioration. MNB capacity to maintain (DO) levels continued the breakdown process, producing more straightforward, less toxic byproducts. MNB are crucial for maintaining DO levels and ROS activity for dye degradation.

Additional research was conducted to investigate the kinetics and progression of the reactions, as illustrated in Fig. 6(a-d) (III) and Table S3. The reaction rates for the photocatalytic degradation of IC and RB5 dyes were compared in the presence and absence of MNB. The results revealed significant differences in reaction rate, underscoring the pivotal role of MNB in enhancing photocatalytic performance. Analysis of the first-order reaction constants demonstrated that MNB significantly facilitated the photocatalytic reaction for TZR, TZ, TR, and ZR. Specifically, the reaction rate was approximately 3.25 × 10−2 min−1 time higher for IC and 4.73 × 10−2 min−1 for RB5 in the presence of MNB. These findings highlight MNB efficacy in promoting more efficient and predictable photocatalytic reactions, demonstrating its potential for enhancing environmental remediation processes. Moreover, the findings of this study demonstrate that the construction of TZR nanocomposites significantly enhances photocatalytic activity compared to previously reported catalysts (as summarized in Table S4) [47–53].

The stability and reusability of the TZR nanocomposite were assessed over three successive cycles for the degradation of IC, RB5 dyes and Carbaryl under UVA light irradiation. As shown in Fig. S5, the TZR nanocomposite's photocatalytic activity is continuously high during the three runs, demonstrating superior structural stability and recyclability. The removal efficiency for IC degradation showed a slight decrease from 99.08 % in Run 1 to 96.99 % in Run 3, indicating a negligible loss of activity (~2.09 %). Similarly, throughout the course of the three cycles, the effectiveness of Carbaryl dropped somewhat from 99.61 % to 94.88 %, while that of RB5 dropped from 98.73 % to 97.17 %. The TZR photocatalyst appears to have significant photochemical durability and resistance to photocorrosion or catalyst deactivation, as seen by its comparatively steady performance across all target contaminants. The slight reduction may be attributed to some factors such as the adsorption of intermediate byproducts on the catalyst surface or minor agglomeration of nanocomposite particles during reuse. However, the TZR nanocomposite's continuously excellent degradation efficiencies throughout repeated cycles demonstrate its promise as a reusable and efficient photocatalyst for environmental remediation.

The dyes IC and RB5 degradation processes were studied using GC–MS to identify and monitor intermediate metabolites. ROS, including H2O2, •OH, and •O2, break aromatic rings and disrupt chromophore structures, causing quick dye loss and significantly decreasing toxicity. The photocatalytic method converted the dyes into more minor, less toxic compounds, proving their efficacy. After 120 min of photodegradation, IC and RB5 degradation produced mostly methylamine and 1,2-ethanediol. as seen in Fig. 7 (a, b). Thus, the analytical procedure is dependable; IC and RB5 have significantly broken aromatic rings after breaking down, indicating simpler molecules. This procedure showed how molecule structure affects degradation efficiency. RB5, with a larger and more complex structure, degraded more slowly than IC. This illustrates how molecular characteristics affect photocatalysis. ROS-driven pathways can significantly lower molecular complexity and toxicity, even for dyes with robust structures.

Fig. 8 illustrates TZR, TZ, TR, and ZR catalysts with and without MNB were compared for photocatalytic degradation of carbaryl insecticides under UVA irradiation (experimental conditions 1–8). Carbaryl concentration dropped considerably after 120 min, culminating at 99.60 % in the first set of experiments (TZR + UVA + MNB), which degraded fastest. Conditions 2–4 had degradation efficiencies of 93.06, 78.20, and 81.58 %. Without MNB, degradation efficiency was lowest at 5–8, with maximum values of 97.49, 65.71, 49.75, and 74.94 %. MNB increases degradation by increasing active sites, surface area, and adsorption capacity, hence average performance is related with MNB missing, the experimental design increased oxygen production, decreased treatment time, and raised water oxygen saturation, improving photocatalytic process efficiency. MNB and UV irradiation enhance pesticide breakdown, making them more effective than either alone.

Fig. 8 illustrates that TZR photocatalysts were tested for carbaryl degradation using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. The spectra showed a 220 nm absorption peak, indicating carbaryl presence. UVA irradiation reduced this peak, indicating photocatalyst and MNB carbaryl degradation. Under experimental conditions 1 and 5, degradation patterns resembled dye degradation, suggesting consistency. This consistency means that significant characteristics like component presence or absence impacted photocatalytic degradation.

The pseudo-first-order kinetic model was used in Fig. 8 for degrading kinetic investigation. The rate constants (k) for conditions 1–4 with MNB were 4.73, 2.01, 1.21 × 10−2, and 8.70 × 10−3 min−1, with R2 values of 0.998, 0.957, 0.965, and 0.946. The first condition (TZR + UVA + MNB) exhibited the best model fit (R2 = 0.999) and significant rate constant (k = 4.73 × 10−2 min−1). This indicates rapid degradation. In comparison, conditions 5–8 without MNB had R2 values of 0.983, 0.981, and 0.976. The rate constants (k) for treating RB5 without MNB were 3.21, 1.73 × 10−2, 9.30 × 10−3, and 5.40 × 10−3 min−1, with R2 values of 0.989, 0.991, and 0.996. Linear pseudo-first-order kinetic graphs examined photocatalytic carbaryl pesticide degradation. Comparing MNB-free and MNB-added treatments to conditions 1 and 5. Using the pseudo-first-order kinetic model, the reaction rate constants (k) for photocatalytic degradation with MNB condition 5 (TZR + UVA) were 1.52 times greater than without MNB. MNB greatly enhances photocatalytic carbaryl breakdown under UVA light. Photocatalytic carbaryl degradation was greatly enhanced by MNB aeration.

The TOC degradation of the dyes and pesticide was investigated after 120 min of photocatalytic degradation using TZR with MNB under UVA irradiation. TOC analysis revealed removal efficiencies of 25.42 % for IC, 15.92 % for RB5, and 20.87 % for carbaryl, indicating substantial degradation of the initial organic content. These values correspond to TOC retentions of 74.58 % for IC, 84.52 % for RB5, and 83.70 % for carbaryl, highlighting incomplete mineralization. The results suggest that while significant degradation occurred, the complex aromatic structures of these pollutants are largely resistant to complete mineralization. For carbaryl, the observed TOC reduction is primarily attributed to the cleavage of carbamate ester bonds, yielding intermediate compounds that retain organic carbon [54,55]. In contrast, despite the resistance of IC's aromatic sulfonate group and RB5's stable azo chromophore to oxidation, degradation of their smaller, structurally simpler intermediates was confirmed through GC–MS analysis [56–58]. These findings underscore the complexity of pollutant mineralization in photocatalytic water treatment, emphasizing the distinction between partial organic removal and full mineralization into environmentally benign end products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photocatalytic mechanism for TZR NCs with MNB
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the results, the proposed photocatalytic mechanism for TZR NCs is depicted on Fig. 9 . Understanding the key factors that improve the photocatalytic efficiency of these NCs is crucial. The band edge positions of TiO2 and ZnO were calculated using the Mulliken-Jaffe equation (Eq. 2 and 3), offering valuable insights into their role in enhancing overall photocatalytic performance [59].
(2)
E CB
= χ −
E e
− 0 .
5E g
(3)
E VB
=
E CB
+
E g

The parameters χ, Ee, ECB, EVB, and Eg represent the semiconductor electronegativity on the Mulliken-Jaffe scale, free electron energy on the hydrogen scale (4.5 eV), conduction band (CB) potential, valence band (VB) potential, and semiconductor bandgap, respectively. When exposed to light, the TZR NCs composite produces electron-hole pairs, with electrons (e−) in conduction bands (CB) and holes (h+) in valence bands (VB) [60]. Photogenerated electrons in CB of ZnO (−0.27 eV vs. NHE) effectively move to RGO, recombining with holes in VB of TiO2 (+2.77 eV vs. NHE). This mechanism leaves behind electrons and holes in ZnO and TiO2, which actively engage in reduction and oxidation processes. The electrons in CB of TiO2 (−0.15 eV vs. NHE) have great reducing power and can generate superoxide radical anions •O2 − by reducing adsorbed O2 molecules. Meanwhile, holes (h+) in the VB of ZnO (+2.85 eV vs. NHE) with a high oxidation potential lead to the production of hydroxyl radicals •OH which play an important role in the breakdown of pollutant molecules. RGO, with its high conductivity and prolonged conjugation structure, acts as an electron acceptor, reducing charge-carrier recombination. Furthermore, RGO improves both light absorption capacity and organic pollutant adsorption on the photocatalyst surface, resulting in increased pollutants breakdown performance [61].

The antibacterial effectiveness of the TZR photocatalyst was examined by measuring the inhibition zones produced against E. coli and S. aureus at different concentrations (25, 50, and 75 μgL−1) under visible light. As shown in Fig. 10 , the zones of inhibition (ZOI) increased with higher concentrations of TZR photocatalyst, demonstrating a dose-dependent antibacterial activity. In comparison, amoxicillin was used as a positive control, which exhibited the highest zones of inhibition, while DMSO, serving as the negative control, displayed no inhibitory effect. The quantitative analysis of inhibition zones (Fig. 10(a,b)) confirmed that the antibacterial activity of TZR photocatalyst was more pronounced against S. aureus than against E. coli. However, the inhibition zones were 4 mm, 6 mm, and 10 mm at 25, 50, and 75 μgL−1, respectively for E. coli, while for S. aureus, the zones were 6 mm, 9 mm, and 12 mm at the same concentrations [62].

At 75 μgL−1, the inhibition zones for S. aureus were significantly larger than those for E. coli, indicating that S. aureus is more susceptible to TZR photocatalyst under the given conditions. Moreover, Amoxicillin used as the positive control, exhibited the largest inhibition zones of 18 mm for E. coli and 16 mm for S. aureus, confirming its superior antibacterial potency. This differential response can be attributed to structural differences in bacterial cell walls. Gram-positive bacteria like S. aureus possess a thick peptidoglycan layer but lack an outer membrane, allowing easier penetration and interaction of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by the TZR photocatalyst [63,64]. In contrast, the outer membrane of Gram-negative E. coli acts as a barrier, reducing photocatalyst efficacy [65]. Therefore, DMSO, serving as the negative control, showed no inhibition, highlighting the specificity of the antibacterial activity of the TZR photocatalyst [66]. These findings align with literature reporting enhanced photocatalytic antibacterial activity via ROS-mediated membrane damage and intracellular component oxidation [67].

In summary, we report on the fabrication of a TZR photocatalyst using a simple hydrothermal method and demonstrate its high efficiency for photocatalytic applications. The combination of TiO2, ZnO, and rGO has been confirmed through detailed structural and surface characterization, resulting in higher light absorption, effective charge carrier separation, and better surface reactivity. The photocatalytic activity of the TZR NCs exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance, particularly when combined with MNB in degrading synthetic dyes (IC and RB5) and carbaryl pesticide, achieving near-complete removal under UVA irradiation. Beyond chemical pollutant degradation, the TZR NCs demonstrated considerable antibacterial activity against S. aureus and E. coli, highlighting their potential for reducing biological pollutants in water systems. This study not only demonstrates the synergistic effects of rGO and MNB on photocatalytic and antibacterial performance, but it also offers an effective and scalable wastewater treatment approach that targets both organic pollutants and harmful bacteria. Future study should look at extending this photocatalyst system to visible-light-driven applications, real industrial wastewater samples, and further optimizing the MNB-assisted process to increase long-term stability and reusability under different environmental circumstances.
